text: The video shows the standard trocar placement for robotic repair of a large paraesophageal hernia, with 8mm robotic trocars and a liver retractor positioned in the subxiphoid space.

The stomach is visible herniating through the crura, with the liver and diaphragm also in view. The surgeon reduces the herniated stomach from above the diaphragmatic hiatus into the chest, revealing chronic irritation and thickening at the diaphragmatic crura, particularly on the anterior portion.

The surgeon opens the pars flaccida to expose the right crus of the diaphragm. An orogastric tube is used to deflate the air-filled stomach. The peritoneum overlying the right crus is partially opened to access the hernia sac, revealing the underlying muscle. The dissection proceeds over the anterior diaphragmatic hiatus, freeing the hernia sac from surrounding structures, including the lung pleura, and continues to the left side.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to further expose the left crus of the diaphragm, creating a window. The greater curvature of the stomach is opened and extended towards the left crus. The hernia sac is visible on the anterior portion of the gastroesophageal junction. The view includes the right and left crura and the esophagus passing through the diaphragmatic hiatus. The hernia sac is dissected off the anterior portion of the stomach and onto the esophagus for removal from the abdomen.

The anterior vagus nerve is clearly visualized and preserved without injury during the procedure. The posterior vagus nerve is also preserved. The hernia sac is dissected and removed entirely from the abdomen.

A Penrose drain is passed around the gastroesophageal junction and secured with an endo loop, providing retraction for the dissection of the esophagus within the mediastinum. The dissection is performed circumferentially around the esophagus to achieve sufficient laxity of the gastroesophageal junction below the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon navigates through extensive chronic scarring within the mediastinum, retracting the esophagus and stomach. Posterior dissection reveals critical structures such as the aorta, right and left pleura, and the esophagus at the top of the screen. The gastroesophageal junction is positioned over three centimeters below the diaphragmatic hiatus without downward tension. Sutures are introduced to secure the position.
The surgeon begins the hiatal closure using a unidirectional barbed permanent suture, specifically a proline stratific suture, to secure the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon takes large bites of the crura for hiatal closure, using a large grasping forcep to size the closure. This technique aims to minimize recurrence risk while ensuring sufficient laxity to prevent swallowing difficulties.

The surgeon initiates the fundoplication by bringing the fundus posterior to the esophagus and performing a shoeshine maneuver to ensure sufficient laxity. Three sutures are placed from the stomach to the hiatal closure, forming a Toupee fundoplication.

The surgeon performs a 270-degree partial wrap, known as a toupee fundoplication, using slip knots to apply sufficient tension on the sutures. This technique is chosen to balance reflux control with a lower risk of side effects compared to a complete 360-degree wrap.
The surgeon places permanent sutures from the esophagus to the stomach at the ten o'clock position, carefully avoiding the anterior vagus nerves. A slip knot is used to secure the sutures.

The surgeon places three to four sutures from the stomach to the esophagus at the two o'clock position. A forced suture is placed from the stomach to the upper portion of the stomach at the gastroesophageal junction. The final suture is placed from the left cruce to the stomach posteriorly.

The surgeon cuts and removes the Penrose drain, followed by the removal of needles from the abdomen. The wrap is visualized, and the liver retractor is taken out, marking the completion of the procedure.
